titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Will Irwin and Inez Haynes Gillmore Papers
Irwin (Will) and Inez Haynes Gillmore Papers
titlestmt.author: by Danijela True and Jennifer Meehan

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 3750953.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday March 28, 2018 at 23:12

did.unittitle: Will Irwin and Inez Haynes Gillmore papers

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection consists primarly of correspondence and diaries, as well as a small amount of writings, photographs, and clippings, documenting the life and work of Inez Haynes Irwin and Will Irwin.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Mrs. Eben Given, 1964. Gift of Jane Parker, 1981.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Will Irwin and Inez Haynes Gillmore Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Will Irwin and Inez Haynes Gillmore Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
The collection received initial processing at or around the time of acquisition. Further rehousing and description were carried out in 2012.
Formerly classed as: Za Irwin, Uncat Za Irwin.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

bioghist.head: Will Irwin (1873-1948)
bioghist.p: Will Irwin, American journalist and author. Irwin was born September 14, 1873, in Oneida, New York. He received a B.A. from Stanford University in 1899. He worked for a number of years as a newspaper report at the San Francisco Wave, the San Francisco Chronicle and the New York Sun, and then for a year as a writing editor at McClure's in 1906.

Irwin then spent a number of years as a free-lance journalist and fiction writer. In 1915 he went abroad as a war correspondent and covered World War I action for both British and American periodicals. Following several years of observing and reporting on the war, Irwin returned to the United States in 1920. He thereafter wrote numerous anti-war publications, among them his best-selling book The Next War (1921). His other books include Christ or Mars? (1923), Herbert Hoover: A Reminiscent Biography (1928), Propaganda and the News; or, What Makes You Think So? (1936), his autobiographical Making of a Reporter (1942), and The American Newspaper (1969).
Irwin died of cerebral occlusion, February 24, 1948, in Greenwich Village, New York.
"Will(iam) (Henry) Irwin." Contemporary Authors Online. Detroit: Gale, 2003. Gale Biography In Context. Web. 21 Aug. 2012.

bioghist.head: Inez Haynes Gillmore Irwin (1873-1970)
bioghist.p: Inez Haynes Irwin, American author and political activist. Haynes Irwin was born March 2, 1873, in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. She attended Radcliffe College from 1897-1900. Haynes Irwin was an ardent supporter of women's suffrage and co-founded the National Collegiate Equal Suffrage League. She was also a member of the National Advisory Council of the National Women's party. Haynes Irwin's book, The Story of the Women's Party, is an account of the history and activities of this political organization.

In her writing, Haynes Irwin confronted the issues of divorce, single-parenthood, unwed motherhood, and women in the work force. Her works include: Phoebe and Ernest (1910), The Lady of Kingdoms (1917), Gertrude Haviland's Divorce (1925), and Angels and Amazons: A Hundred Years of American Women (1933).
Haynes Irwin married Rufus Hamilton Gillmore in 1897; that marriage ended. She married Will Irwin in 1916.
"Inez Haynes Irwin." Contemporary Authors Online. Detroit: Gale, 2002. Gale Biography In Context. Web. 21 Aug. 2012.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists primarly of correspondence and diaries, as well as a small amount of writings, photographs, and clippings, documenting the life and work of Inez Haynes Irwin and Will Irwin.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Correspondence, 1905-1931. II. Diaries, 1917-1961. III. Other Papers, 1887-1945.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence consists primarily of letters received by Inez Haynes Irwin from Will Irwin.

did.unittitle: Letters from Phyllis Given to her mother Maud Duganne

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Inez Haynes Irwin Diaries and Will Irwin Diaries.

did.unittitle: "The Hamadryads, A Masque of Apollo in One Act by Will H. Irwin," typescript
"Midsummer High Jinks, 1904, The Hamadryads by Will Irwin," photograph album
Letters from Will Irwin to Phyllis Given
Poems written by Will Irwin when he was at Stanford University, typescript
Writings by Inez Haynes Irwin, typescripts
Diary of Inez Haynes Irwin on her first visit to Paris
